html:
Carlisle SynTec Systems on Laredo Commercial Roofs

Carlisle SynTec is one of the manufacturers whose membrane systems show up regularly on our proposals, particularly on TPO and EPDM jobs. The company is based in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and operates as part of Carlisle Companies, a large diversified building-products manufacturer. We work with their product lines the same way we work with several other manufacturers - matching the system to the building, not the building to a preferred brand.

Where the Company Started

Carlisle SynTec built its reputation initially on EPDM rubber roofing, a product category the broader Carlisle organization was an early and significant manufacturer of decades ago. That EPDM history is part of why the company's rubber membrane line remains a substantial part of its current commercial roofing catalog, alongside newer TPO and PVC product lines developed as the single-ply market shifted toward those materials.

The Product Lines We Spec

Carlisle's current commercial catalog spans EPDM, TPO, and PVC single-ply membranes in various thicknesses, along with insulation, fastening accessories, and coating products meant to work as a complete roof assembly rather than a membrane sold in isolation. On a Laredo job we'll pull from whichever part of that catalog fits the building - a white TPO membrane for a warehouse roof, an EPDM system for a building with deck-movement concerns, and so on.

Insulation and Accessory Products Alongside the Membrane

Beyond membrane itself, Carlisle manufactures a full line of insulation board, cover boards, fastening plates, and edge metal designed to be specified together as a single documented system rather than sourced piecemeal from different suppliers. Specifying components from one manufacturer's compatible catalog simplifies the warranty conversation considerably, since a mixed-source assembly can raise questions about which company is responsible if a failure occurs at the interface between two different manufacturers' products. We default to matched systems for exactly that reason unless there's a specific cost or availability issue that argues otherwise.

EPDM Roots and What Changed

The broader single-ply roofing market has moved substantially toward TPO over the last couple of decades, largely for reflectivity and heat-welded seam reasons that matter a lot in a climate like Laredo's. Carlisle's EPDM line remains available and supported, but the majority of new commercial roofing specifications we write today, across manufacturers generally, lean toward TPO or PVC rather than EPDM, for the reasons covered on our EPDM roof-systems page.

Reading a Carlisle Warranty Document

Manufacturer warranties on single-ply roofing typically cover the membrane material itself against manufacturing defects for a specified term, with separate terms sometimes available for labor or full-system coverage depending on how the project is contracted and installed. The specific terms, exclusions, and required maintenance obligations vary by product line and by the individual project's documentation, so we review the actual warranty language with the owner rather than summarizing it generically - warranty terms are a legal document, not a marketing summary, and we treat them that way.

  • Membrane type and thickness matched to the building's traffic and exposure profile
  • Insulation and fastening components sourced as a compatible system, not mixed ad hoc
  • Installation documented to the standard the manufacturer's warranty terms require
  • Warranty registration paperwork filed promptly after project completion
  • Product data sheets kept on file for the owner's future reference

Fitting Carlisle Into a Laredo Job

We don't default to any single manufacturer regardless of the building. Carlisle's product range is broad enough to cover most of what we spec on a typical Laredo commercial roof, and their distribution and product data availability make it straightforward to document a job to their specification. Where a different manufacturer's specific product - a particular coating chemistry, a specific PVC formulation - fits a building's needs better, we'll spec that instead.

On cross-dock warehouse projects in Killam and Unitec specifically, Carlisle's fully documented TPO system - membrane, insulation, and fastening all specified together - is a common baseline we compare other manufacturers' proposals against, simply because it's well established and thoroughly documented in the field.

On smaller retail and QSR jobs, cost and current local distributor stock usually matter more than any specific brand advantage, and we'll shop Carlisle against Versico, GAF, or another manufacturer on those projects rather than assuming Carlisle is automatically the least expensive option.

Questions About Carlisle SynTec Systems

Is Carlisle the manufacturer you recommend most often?

We spec from several manufacturers depending on the building and the specific product needed; Carlisle's broad single-ply catalog makes it a frequent fit, but not an automatic default.

Does Carlisle still make EPDM roofing?

Yes, EPDM remains part of their current product catalog alongside TPO and PVC, though most new commercial specifications we write today lean toward TPO or PVC for the climate-specific reasons discussed on our roof-systems pages.

What does a Carlisle product warranty actually cover?

That depends on the specific product line and project documentation; we review the actual warranty terms with the owner rather than generalizing, since exclusions and maintenance requirements vary by product.

Can Carlisle products be mixed with another manufacturer's insulation or fasteners?

We generally source components as a matched system per the manufacturer's specification, since mixing components across manufacturers can create warranty complications that aren't worth the marginal cost savings.

Where does Carlisle SynTec manufacture its products?

Carlisle SynTec is headquartered in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and operates as part of the larger Carlisle Companies building-products organization, which also owns Versico as a separate roofing brand.
